perturbation.original_text: 2.2 Early Termination This Agreement may be terminated as follows: (1) Tsinghua Unigroup and ChipMOS Taiwan terminate this Agreement by mutual agreement in writing; (2) In the event that Tsinghua Unigroup or ChipMOS materially breaches this Agreement and such breach is incurable, the other Party may immediately terminate this Agreement by giving written notice to the breaching Party; if such breach is curable, this Agreement will be terminated automatically after ten (10) days from the date on which the breaching Party received the written notice given by the other Party, if the breaching Party fails to cure such breach; or (3) This Agreement shall be simultaneously terminated, rescinded or become invalid upon the termination, rescission, or invalidation of the Share Subscription Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 Early Termination This Agreement may be terminated as follows: (1) Tsinghua Unigroup and ChipMOS Taiwan terminate this Agreement by mutual agreement in writing; (2) In the event that Tsinghua Unigroup or ChipMOS materially breaches this Agreement. The other party may attempt to seek resolution through negotiation. Termination for breach will only be considered if the breach persists after a 90-day negotiation period; or (3) This Agreement shall be simultaneously terminated, rescinded or become invalid upon the termination, rescission, or invalidation of the Share Subscription Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows immediate termination for incurable breaches or termination after a 10-day cure period for curable breaches. The modified text mandates a 90-day negotiation period before termination can even be considered, contradicting the original immediacy and creates uncertainty in the conditions that constitute an early termination under the contract, particulary with the mention of material breach of agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2 Early Termination This Agreement may be terminated as follows: (1) Tsinghua Unigroup and ChipMOS Taiwan terminate this Agreement by mutual agreement in writing; (2) In the event that Tsinghua Unigroup or ChipMOS materially breaches this Agreement. The other party may attempt to seek resolution through negotiation. Termination for breach will only be considered if the breach persists after a 90-day negotiation period; or (3) This Agreement shall be simultaneously terminated, rescinded or become invalid upon the termination, rescission, or invalidation of the Share Subscription Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows immediate termination for incurable breaches or termination after a 10-day cure period for curable breaches. The changed text mandates a 90-day negotiation period before termination can even be considered, contradicting the original immediacy and creates uncertainty in the conditions that constitute an early termination under the contract, particulary with the mention of material breach of agreement. This adheres to the definition of an in-text contradiction, specifically 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with the laws of Taiwan. The Parties shall first seek to solve any dispute arising out of or related to this Agreement through negotiation. If the Parties fail to solve such dispute through negotiation, each Party shall have the right to issue notice ("Dispute Notice") to the other Party, and such Dispute Notice shall include the content of the dispute. If the Parties fail to resolve such dispute amicably through negotiation within sixty (60) days from the date on which a Party issues its Dispute Notice to the other Party, each Party shall have the right to submit such dispute to the Hong Kong International Arbitration Center, and proceed with the arbitration procedures in accordance with the Rules of the International Chamber of Commerce with three (3) arbitrators. Each Party shall each select one (1) arbitrator, and the third arbitrator shall be appointed by the two (2) arbitrators so selected. All language used in such proceedings shall be Mandarin Chinese. The Parties agree to keep the content of the dispute and the proceeding of the arbitration confidential. The arbitration award shall be final and binding on the Parties. The losing Party in such arbitration shall bear all of the costs and expenses related to the arbitration as determined by the arbitrators in such dispute (including attorney's fees).
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with the laws of Taiwan, except for disputes relating to intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China. The Parties shall first seek to solve any dispute arising out of or related to this Agreement through negotiation. If the Parties fail to solve such dispute through negotiation, each Party shall have the right to issue notice ("Dispute Notice") to the other Party, and such Dispute Notice shall include the content of the dispute. If the Parties fail to resolve such dispute amicably through negotiation within sixty (60) days from the date on which a Party issues its Dispute Notice to the other Party, each Party shall have the right to submit such dispute to the Hong Kong International Arbitration Center, and proceed with the arbitration procedures in accordance with the Rules of the International Chamber of Commerce with three (3) arbitrators. Each Party shall each select one (1) arbitrator, and the third arbitrator shall be appointed by the two (2) arbitrators so selected. All language used in such proceedings shall be Mandarin Chinese. The Parties agree to keep the content of the dispute and the proceeding of the arbitration confidential. The arbitration award shall be final and binding on the Parties. The losing Party in such arbitration shall bear all of the costs and expenses related to the arbitration as determined by the arbitrators in such dispute (including attorney's fees).
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the agreement is governed by the laws of Taiwan. However, the modified text introduces an exception, stating that disputes related to intellectual property shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China. This creates an in-text contradiction within the same section. The contradiction creates uncertainty in determining which jurisdiction's laws apply to disputes involving intellectual property, and this is further complicated by the original statement specifying Taiwanese law for all disputes related to the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with the laws of Taiwan, except for disputes relating to intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China. The Parties shall first seek to solve any dispute arising out of or related to this Agreement through negotiation. If the Parties fail to solve such dispute through negotiation, each Party shall have the right to issue notice ("Dispute Notice") to the other Party, and such Dispute Notice shall include the content of the dispute. If the Parties fail to resolve such dispute amicably through negotiation within sixty (60) days from the date on which a Party issues its Dispute Notice to the other Party, each Party shall have the right to submit such dispute to the Hong Kong International Arbitration Center, and proceed with the arbitration procedures in accordance with the Rules of the International Chamber of Commerce with three (3) arbitrators. Each Party shall each select one (1) arbitrator, and the third arbitrator shall be appointed by the two (2) arbitrators so selected. All language used in such proceedings shall be Mandarin Chinese. The Parties agree to keep the content of the dispute and the proceeding of the arbitration confidential. The arbitration award shall be final and binding on the Parties. The losing Party in such arbitration shall bear all of the costs and expenses related to the arbitration as determined by the arbitrators in such dispute (including attorney's fees).
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the governing law by stating that intellectual property disputes are governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China, while the original text states that the entire agreement is governed by the laws of Taiwan. This creates a clear and direct contradiction regarding which jurisdiction's laws apply to intellectual property disputes, creating ambiguity and potential unenforceability as per the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.10 Actual Performance The Parties acknowledge and agree that if any of the provisions provided herein are not performed in accordance with the specific terms and conditions or are otherwise violated, this will cause irreparable damages for which monetary compensation would not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, the Parties agrees that, in addition to any other remedies available in common law or equity, each Party shall be entitled to seek injunction and other equitable remedies, including the actual performance of the terms and conditions provided herein, and it is not necessary to post any bond or other security.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.10 Actual Performance The Parties acknowledge and agree that if any of the provisions provided herein are not performed in accordance with the specific terms and conditions or are otherwise violated, this will cause irreparable damages for which monetary compensation would not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, the Parties agree that, in addition to any other remedies available in common law or equity, each Party may seek injunction and other equitable remedies, but shall first attempt to quantify damages and seek monetary compensation before pursuing equitable relief. Posting a bond or other security may be required at the discretion of the court.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that a party is 'entitled to seek injunction and other equitable remedies' without needing to post a bond. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that a party 'may seek injunction and other equitable remedies, but shall first attempt to quantify damages and seek monetary compensation', and makes posting a bond discretionary to the court. This contradicts the original entitlement and creates uncertainty about the availability of equitable remedies. and thus introduces an in-text contradiction about when equitable relief can be sought.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 Actual Performance The Parties acknowledge and agree that if any of the provisions provided herein are not performed in accordance with the specific terms and conditions or are otherwise violated, this will cause irreparable damages for which monetary compensation would not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, the Parties agree that, in addition to any other remedies available in common law or equity, each Party may seek injunction and other equitable remedies, but shall first attempt to quantify damages and seek monetary compensation before pursuing equitable relief. Posting a bond or other security may be required at the discretion of the court.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by making equitable remedies contingent on first seeking monetary compensation and discretionary court approval for posting a bond. This directly conflicts with the original clause in the same section which states that each party is 'entitled to seek injunction and other equitable remedies' without needing to post a bond. The new requirement and discretionary bond posting directly contradicts the initial entitlement, thus creating an in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' definition, as the availability and conditions for seeking equitable relief are no longer consistently defined within the same section.
